message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it. Read the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and find the code path / contract / test that makes the PoC impossible or the concern moot. Only conclude survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

Finding P-4: No test asserts the numpy resample-stability path's memory bound, and the pure↔numpy equivalence test uses only tiny inputs
File: src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:305
Description: Test-quality gap tied to P-1: `test_resample_stability_numpy_path_matches_pure_python` validates numerical equivalence only on small arrays (null=40, res=25). There is no test exercising the path at any size that would surface the R×N allocation, and no test pinning the documented contract that the numpy path is 'purely a speed optimization, never a semantic change' against the memory dimension. As a result a future refactor to a chunked/searchsorted implementation (the P-1 fix) has no guardrail proving it preserves results at scale, and the current quadratic-memory behavior is invisible to the suite. This is the kind of coverage hole a discrimination auditor focused on correctness would miss because the existing test passes.
PoC: # The only equivalence test (tests/test_confirm.py:191) uses null=40, res=25 -- far below any
# size where the R x N boolean mask is observable. No test covers large-R/large-N parity or any
# memory characteristic.

Return survives=false if you can refute it (with the specific reason), survives=true if it withstands a genuine disproof attempt.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

270	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
271	) -> Verdict:
272	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + measured stability.
273	
274	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
275	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
276	
277	    The predicted tail is taken from the hypothesis's pre-registered ``predicted_direction``
278	    (issue #24). The pattern must (a) beat the null at ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and
279	    (b) be **stable across resamples** — measured by the engine from ``resample_statistics``,
280	    not asserted by the caller (audit pass 2, issue #21). Outcomes:
281	
282	    - significant in the predicted tail AND stable → ``supported``;
283	    - significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``;
284	    - unstable, not significant, or stability not assessed → ``indeterminate``.
285	
286	    If ``resample_statistics`` is omitted, stability cannot be measured, so ``supported`` is
287	    unavailable and the verdict is ``indeterminate`` ("stability not assessed").
288	    """
289	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
290	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
291	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
292	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
293	    _validate_finite("observed", observed)
294	    if not null_distribution:
295	        raise ValueError(
296	            "a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation"
297	        )
298	    for v in null_distribution:
299	        _validate_finite("null_distribution value", v)
300	    if not (0.0 < stability_threshold <= 1.0):
301	        raise ValueError(f"stability_threshold must be in (0, 1]; got {stability_threshold}")
302	
303	    direction = _resolve_direction(hypothesis, predicted_direction)
304	    opposite: Direction = "less" if direction == "greater" else "greater"
305	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, direction)
306	    p_contra = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, opposite)
307	    n = len(null_distribution)
308	
309	    stability: float | None = None
310	    if resample_statistics is not None:
311	        if not resample_statistics:
312	            raise ValueError("resample_statistics, if given, must be non-empty")
313	        for r in resample_statistics:
314	            _validate_finite("resample statistic", r)
315	        stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)
316	    stable = stability is not None and stability >= stability_threshold
317	
318	    rule = (
319	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail '{direction}'; "
320	        f"stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold} (engine-measured)"
321	    )
322	    evidence = {
323	        "p_support": p_support,
324	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
325	        "null_n": n,
326	        "predicted_direction": direction,
327	        "stability": stability,
328	        "stability_threshold": stability_threshold,
329	        "n_resamples": len(resample_statistics) if resample_statistics is not None else 0,
330	    }
331	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
332	
333	    # A contradiction (significant in the opposite tail) is reportable without stability.
334	    if p_contra <= alpha:
335	        return Verdict.contradicted(
336	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="significant in the opposite tail", **common
337	        )
338	    if stability is None:
339	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
340	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
341	            rule,
342	            notes="stability not assessed (no resamples supplied)",
343	            **common,
344	        )
345	    if not stable:
346	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
347	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
348	        )
349	    if p_support <= alpha:
350	        return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
351	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
352	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
353	    )

191	def test_resample_stability_numpy_path_matches_pure_python():
192	    # #F-017: the numpy-vectorized stability computation must return EXACTLY the pure-Python
193	    # value (identical integer counts + (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha test) — it is a speed
194	    # optimization, never a semantic change.
195	    import random
196	
197	    from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import _empirical_p, _resample_stability
198	
199	    def _pure(resamples, null, tail, alpha):
200	        return sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha) / len(resamples)
201	
202	    rng = random.Random(7)
203	    for _ in range(50):
204	        null = [rng.gauss(0, 1) for _ in range(40)]
205	        res = [rng.gauss(0.4, 1) for _ in range(25)]
206	        for tail in ("greater", "less"):
207	            for alpha in (0.01, 0.05, 0.2):
208	                assert _resample_stability(res, null, tail, alpha) == _pure(res, null, tail, alpha)

content.text: Let me read the full `_resample_stability` implementation to confirm the R×N allocation and the documented contract.

content.content:
192	def _resample_stability(
193	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
194	) -> float:
195	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
196	
197	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
198	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
199	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
200	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
201	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
202	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
203	
204	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
205	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
206	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
207	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
208	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
209	    """
210	    n = len(null)
211	    try:
212	        import numpy as np
213	
214	        null_arr = np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64)
215	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
216	        if tail == "greater":
217	            extreme = (null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)
218	        else:
219	            extreme = (null_arr[None, :] <= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)
220	        p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
221	        return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
222	    except ImportError:
223	        significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
224	        return significant / len(resamples)
225	
226	
227	_VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
228	
229	
230	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
231	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time
232	    argument is optional and, if given, must match the pre-registered one.
233	
234	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}`` and any other value is
235	    rejected: an unrecognized direction would otherwise fall through to the 'less' tail in
236	    :func:`_empirical_p`, silently flipping supported↔contradicted (audit pass 2, #128).
237	    """
238	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
239	    if locked_dir is not None:
240	        if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
241	            raise ValueError(
242	                "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
243	                f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
244	            )
245	        resolved = locked_dir
246	    elif predicted_direction is None:
247	        raise ValueError(
248	            "no predicted_direction: set it on the hypothesis at pre-registration "
249	            "(preferred) or pass it explicitly"
250	        )
251	    else:
252	        resolved = predicted_direction
253	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
254	        raise ValueError(
255	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
256	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
257	        )
258	    return resolved
259	
260	
261	def confirm_whole_corpus(

content.text:
The finding is a test-coverage gap claim. Let me verify each of its specific assertions against the actual code.

The finding claims:
1. The only equivalence test uses null=40, res=25 — **Confirmed** (test_confirm.py:204-205).
2. No test exercises the path at a size that surfaces R×N allocation — **Confirmed** (only that one test references `_resample_stability`; no large-R/large-N variant).
3. No test pins the documented "purely a speed optimization, never a semantic change" contract against the memory dimension — **Confirmed**. The docstring (confirm.py:208) makes this claim, the numpy path materializes `null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]` (a full R×N boolean array, line 217/219), and no test asserts any memory bound or chunked behavior.

Let me check whether any property-based or hypothesis test elsewhere might cover scaled inputs, and whether the code actually does allocate R×N (no chunking).

content.text:
All matches for "hypothesis" are the domain `Hypothesis` type, not property-based testing. There is no scaled, property-based, or memory-bound test. The numpy code at confirm.py:217/219 unconditionally builds a full R×N boolean mask with no chunking.

Every claim in the finding is independently verified against the actual code:
- The single equivalence test (test_confirm.py:191-208) uses null=40, res=25 — far below any size where R×N allocation matters.
- `_resample_stability` is referenced in exactly one test (grep confirmed only line 197/208).
- The docstring explicitly claims "purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change" (confirm.py:208), yet no test pins that contract at scale or against memory; the numpy path materializes a full R×N array (lines 217, 219) with no chunking/searchsorted guard.

The finding is a correctly-characterized coverage gap. I cannot disprove it.

input.reasoning:
Every claim in the finding is verified against the actual code and survives an adversarial disproof attempt.

1) The only equivalence test, test_resample_stability_numpy_path_matches_pure_python (tests/test_confirm.py:191-208), uses null = 40 elements and res = 25 elements (lines 204-205), exactly as the finding states — far below any size where the R×N boolean mask would be observable.

2) _resample_stability is referenced in exactly one test. grep across the whole tests/ tree returns only test_confirm.py:197 (import) and :208 (the equivalence assertion). No test exercises the numpy path at large R / large N, and no property-based test exists (every "hypothesis" hit is the domain Hypothesis type, not Hypothesis-the-library; no @given, tracemalloc, nbytes, or scale fixtures).

3) The numpy path in confirm.py:216-221 unconditionally materializes a full R×N boolean array: `null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]` (line 217, and the <= mirror on 219), with no chunking or searchsorted. The docstring at confirm.py:208 explicitly asserts this is "purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change," yet no test pins that contract at scale or against any memory dimension. A future refactor to a chunked/searchsorted implementation (the P-1 fix) would have no guardrail proving result-parity at scale, exactly as the finding claims.

I could not find any code path, contract, or test that makes the PoC impossible or the concern moot. The finding accurately describes a real test-coverage hole.

content.text:
The finding survives. All three of its specific claims are confirmed against the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay:

- `tests/test_confirm.py:204-205` — the sole equivalence test uses null=40, res=25.
- `tests/test_confirm.py:191-208` is the only test referencing `_resample_stability` (grep across all of `tests/` confirms this); no large-scale or property-based variant exists.
- `src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:217,219` unconditionally allocates the full R×N boolean mask with no chunking, while the docstring at line 208 claims it is "purely a speed optimization... never a semantic change" — a contract no test pins at scale or against memory.

I could not disprove it: survives=true.
